name: Yakama Nation
alternateNames: Waptailmim (people of the narrows of the river)
language: Yakama (Ichishkíin Sínwit — a Sahaptin language of the Columbia Plateau)
traditionalTerritories: Columbia Plateau from the Cascades east to the Horse Heaven Hills, and the Yakima River watershed from its Cascade headwaters to the Columbia River confluence, Washington State.
culturalNotes: The Yakama Nation is one of the largest tribes in the Pacific Northwest, with a reservation that preserves a significant portion of the Columbia Plateau. Yakama traditional economy was built on the convergence of Columbia River salmon runs and the vast plateau root food resources — particularly camas, cous (biscuitroot), and wapato. The Yakama people are River People whose seasonal round moved between river fishing sites and the plateau root grounds. The 1855 Yakama Treaty preserved significant off-reservation fishing rights, which became central to the 1974 Boldt Decision and subsequent Pacific Northwest fisheries management. The Columbia River is the ecological and cultural thread connecting Yakama territory to the Salish Sea.
heritageSkillRelevance: Columbia and Yakima River salmon fishing — all five Pacific salmon species at traditional fishing sites along the Columbia
Camas root harvesting and preparation — the Yakima Valley camas prairies were among the most productive on the plateau
Cous (biscuitroot, Lomatium) harvesting and drying — a primary protein and carbohydrate source
Tule (bulrush) mat weaving for longhouse coverings — a central Yakama material technology
Columbia Plateau horse culture — the Yakama were among the first plateau peoples to adopt horses; horsemanship knowledge
Ichishkíin Sínwit language revitalization programs
Plateau food preservation — smoking, drying, and pit cooking for winter storage
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Yakama is a Sahaptian-speaking River People of the Columbia Plateau — culturally and linguistically distinct from Coast Salish peoples of the Puget Sound and BC coast. Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or 'Northwest Coast peoples.' The Yakama share salmon economy with coastal peoples but occupy a distinct cultural and ecological zone. Spiritual and ceremonial practices are Level 3-4.

name: Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation (CTUIR)
languageFamily: Sahaptian and Cayuse (Penutian — both Sahaptin and the Cayuse isolate)
language: Umatilla (Imatalamłáma — Sahaptin); Walla Walla (Sahaptin); Cayuse (language isolate — now functionally extinct)
traditionalTerritories: Umatilla and Walla Walla river drainages, the Blue Mountains of northeastern Oregon and southeastern Washington, and the Columbia River at the confluence of the Umatilla and Walla Walla rivers.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation brings together three historically distinct peoples: Cayuse, Umatilla, and Walla Walla. The Cayuse were culturally distinct horse-trading people whose language (Cayuse) is a language isolate now functionally extinct; the Umatilla and Walla Walla speak related Sahaptin dialects. The Blue Mountains region is a rich highland environment between the Columbia Plateau and the Snake River drainage, producing root foods, berries, and game that supplemented the Columbia River salmon economy. The CTUIR has been a leader in salmon restoration on the Columbia River system, including hatchery science and habitat work.
heritageSkillRelevance: Columbia River salmon fishing at traditional Umatilla River and Walla Walla River confluence sites
Plateau root food harvesting — camas, cous, and wapato in the Blue Mountain foothills
Cayuse and Umatilla horse culture — the Cayuse breed (now the Cayuse pony) was developed in this territory
Blue Mountain elk and deer hunting — traditional high-elevation hunting practices
Tule and cattail mat weaving for tipi and longhouse coverings
Umatilla and Walla Walla language documentation efforts
Columbia Plateau trade network participation — Celilo Falls trading was central to CTUIR history
culturalSovereigntyNotes: The CTUIR encompasses three historically distinct peoples — Cayuse, Umatilla, and Walla Walla each have distinct histories, not to be collapsed into one. Sahaptin-speaking peoples are River People of the Columbia Plateau, culturally distinct from Coast Salish. Never aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or generic 'Northwest' peoples.

name: Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs
languageFamily: Sahaptian (Tenino/Warm Springs dialect), Chinookan (Wasco), Numic (Paiute) — three language families in one confederation
language: Warm Springs (Ichishkíin — Sahaptin dialect); Wasco (Upper Chinookan); Northern Paiute (Numic — separate Uto-Aztecan family)
traditionalTerritories: Deschutes River system and the east slopes of the Cascades in north-central Oregon. Traditional territories span from the Columbia River south to the high desert, encompassing the Warm Springs River watershed.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs brings together three linguistically distinct peoples: Warm Springs (Sahaptin-speaking plateau peoples), Wasco (Chinookan-speaking river traders), and Paiute (Numic-speaking Great Basin people). This linguistic diversity reflects the geographic position of Warm Springs — at the junction of the Columbia Plateau, the Cascade Range, and the Great Basin. The Wasco people were the trading nation of the mid-Columbia, operating at the Celilo Falls trading complex before its submersion by the Dalles Dam in 1957. The loss of Celilo Falls is one of the most significant cultural and ecological disruptions in Pacific Northwest history.
heritageSkillRelevance: Columbia and Deschutes River salmon fishing — traditional sites at Celilo Falls (now submerged) and Deschutes River mouth
Plateau root food harvesting — camas, cous, wapato, and kous in the Warm Springs River drainage
Wasco trade canoe construction and Celilo Falls trading history
High desert plant knowledge — sagebrush, bitterroot, and Great Basin medicinal plants (Paiute tradition)
Warm Springs and Wasco language documentation and teaching
Deschutes River canyon rim hunting — pronghorn, deer, and elk in the high desert-plateau interface
Columbia Plateau tule mat weaving and tipi construction traditions
culturalSovereigntyNotes: The Warm Springs confederation encompasses three linguistically distinct peoples — Warm Springs (Sahaptin), Wasco (Chinookan), and Paiute (Numic). Do not treat as homogeneous. None are Coast Salish. The three traditions are distinct and should not be collapsed. Ceremonial protocols for each tradition are Level 3-4.

name: Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation
languageFamily: Salishan (Interior Salish — distinct from Northwest Coast Salishan) and others
language: Multiple interior Salishan and Sahaptian languages — the 12 constituent bands include Okanagan, Sinixt, Nespelem, Sanpoil, Lake (Sinixt), Methow, Wenatchi, Chelan, Entiat, Palus, Moses-Columbia, and Nez Perce
traditionalTerritories: Northeast Washington State, including the Okanagan and Columbia River valleys north and east of the Columbia River bend. Traditional territories of the 12 constituent bands cover a vast area from the Cascades to the Rockies.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation is one of the most diverse tribal confederations in the Pacific Northwest, bringing together 12 constituent bands with distinct languages, histories, and territories. The constituent bands include: Okanagan (Syilx), Sinixt (Arrow Lakes), Nespelem, Sanpoil, Lake, Methow, Wenatchi (Wenatchee), Chelan, Entiat, Palus, Moses-Columbia (Sinkiuse), and Nez Perce (enrolled members). The Columbia River and its tributaries are the ecological spine of Colville territory — salmon connected all 12 bands to a shared economy even as they maintained distinct cultural identities. Grand Coulee Dam (1942) destroyed salmon access to the upper Columbia, a catastrophic loss for the bands whose territories lay above the dam.
heritageSkillRelevance: Columbia River salmon fishing at traditional sites — all 12 bands had rights to specific Columbia River fishing locations
Interior Cascade and plateau root food harvesting — bitterroot (Lewisia), camas, and cous in varied terrain
Okanagan/Syilx and other Interior Salishan language revitalization efforts
Upper Columbia River waterway travel — birch bark canoes for interior river travel
High plateau berry harvesting — huckleberry, serviceberry, and chokecherry drying
Interior plateau hunting traditions — deer, elk, and pronghorn across diverse terrain
Grand Coulee Dam historical context — teaching the catastrophic impact of dam construction on traditional salmon economy
culturalSovereigntyNotes: The 12 constituent bands of the Colville Reservation are distinct peoples — Okanagan/Syilx, Sinixt, Nespelem, Sanpoil, Lake, Methow, Wenatchi, Chelan, Entiat, Palus, Moses-Columbia, and Nez Perce (enrolled). Name the specific band when the context is specific. Interior Salishan is linguistically related to but distinct from Northwest Coast Salishan. Never aggregate as 'Coast Salish.'

name: Spokane Tribe of Indians
languageFamily: Salishan (Interior Salish — Spokane is an Interior Salishan language)
language: Spokane (Spokan — an Interior Salishan language related to Kalispel/Pend d'Oreilles)
traditionalTerritories: Spokane River drainage from the Columbia River confluence upstream to Coeur d'Alene Lake, and the surrounding plateau in northeastern Washington and northern Idaho.
culturalNotes: The Spokane are a River People of the Columbia Plateau whose traditional economy centered on the Spokane River — a significant salmon corridor connecting the Columbia River system to the lakes and mountains of northeastern Washington and northern Idaho. The Spokane Falls (now within the city of Spokane) was a major salmon harvesting and inter-tribal gathering location. Three traditional Spokane divisions existed: Lower Spokane (at the Columbia confluence), Middle Spokane (at the falls), and Upper Spokane (in the higher Spokane Valley). The Spokane tribe currently has legal action for compensation for losses caused by Grand Coulee Dam.
heritageSkillRelevance: Spokane River salmon fishing — chinook and steelhead at the falls and throughout the Spokane River corridor
Spokane Falls gathering and trade — a significant inter-tribal meeting point for Columbia Plateau peoples
Plateau root food harvesting — camas, bitterroot, and cous in the Spokane River plateau
Interior Salishan birch bark canoe construction for river and lake travel
Spokane language revitalization efforts
Plateau berry harvesting and preservation — huckleberry and serviceberry at higher elevations
Deer and elk hunting in the Selkirk Mountains and Spokane River plateau
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Spokane is an Interior Salishan language and people — distinct from both Northwest Coast Salishan and from neighboring Athabascan peoples. Do not conflate Interior Salish with Coast Salish. Salmon economy connects Spokane to the Salish Sea system but their cultural practices are distinct plateau traditions. Never aggregate as 'Coast Salish.'

name: Coeur d'Alene Tribe (Schitsu'umsh)
alternateNames: 'The discovered people' or 'those who are found here'
languageFamily: Salishan (Interior Salish — Schitsu'umsh is an Interior Salishan language)
language: Schitsu'umsh (Coeur d'Alene — an Interior Salishan language related to Spokane and Kalispel)
traditionalTerritories: Coeur d'Alene Lake basin and the surrounding plateau and mountains of northern Idaho, including the St. Joe and Coeur d'Alene River drainages.
culturalNotes: The Schitsu'umsh (Coeur d'Alene) are a lake-centered people whose traditional territory encompasses Coeur d'Alene Lake — one of the largest and most biologically productive lakes in the Columbia Plateau region. The lake provided westslope cutthroat trout, bull trout, and other fish as alternatives and complements to the Columbia River salmon runs. The Coeur d'Alene have been prominent in water quality advocacy for Coeur d'Alene Lake, which was contaminated by mining operations in the late nineteenth and twentieth centuries. The name 'Coeur d'Alene' (French: 'heart of an awl') was applied by French-Canadian fur traders as a comment on perceived sharp trading practices.
heritageSkillRelevance: Coeur d'Alene Lake fishing — westslope cutthroat trout, bull trout, and largemouth bass in the lake ecosystem
Columbia River tributary salmon fishing — chinook and steelhead in the Spokane River and upper Columbia system
Plateau root food harvesting — camas and bitterroot in lake valley meadows
Schitsu'umsh (Coeur d'Alene) language revitalization programs
Interior Salishan birch bark canoe construction and lake navigation
High mountain berry harvesting in the Bitterroot and Coeur d'Alene mountains
Winter count and oral history traditions of the Interior Salishan peoples
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Schitsu'umsh (Coeur d'Alene) is an Interior Salishan people — linguistically related to but culturally distinct from Northwest Coast Salishan peoples. Do not aggregate with coastal peoples. Ceremonial and spiritual protocols are Level 3-4.

name: Kalispel Tribe of Indians (Pend d'Oreilles)
languageFamily: Salishan (Interior Salish — Kalispel is an Interior Salishan language related to Spokane and Coeur d'Alene)
language: Kalispel (Salish-Pend d'Oreilles — an Interior Salishan language)
traditionalTerritories: Pend Oreille River valley from Pend Oreille Lake (northern Idaho) downstream into northeastern Washington and northwestern Montana. Includes the Clark Fork River drainage.
culturalNotes: The Kalispel (also called Pend d'Oreilles — French for 'ear pendants' after traditional ear ornaments) are a river and lake people whose traditional territory follows the Pend Oreille River system from the large Pend Oreille Lake downstream through the Selkirk Mountains to the Columbia River. The Pend Oreille River was a major connectivity corridor for mountain peoples, and Kalispel traditional territory bridges plateau and Rocky Mountain ecological zones. The Kalispel share close linguistic and cultural ties with the Flathead Salish (Bitterroot Salish) and the Coeur d'Alene.
heritageSkillRelevance: Pend Oreille River and Lake fishing — rainbow trout, westslope cutthroat, and Columbia system salmon
Plateau and mountain root food harvesting — bitterroot, camas, and cous in varied mountain-plateau terrain
Kalispel language revitalization programs
Interior mountain canoe construction for Pend Oreille River and lake travel
Mountain goat and bighorn sheep hunting in the Selkirk Mountains
Interior plateau berry harvesting — huckleberry, serviceberry at high elevations
Deer and elk hunting in the Selkirk and Cabinet Mountains
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Kalispel (Pend d'Oreilles) is an Interior Salishan people — distinct from Coast Salish and from neighboring Ktunaxa (Kutenai). Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish.' Ceremonial protocols are Level 3-4.

name: Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes (Flathead Reservation)
languageFamily: Salishan (Bitterroot Salish — Interior Salish) and Kutenai (language isolate — unrelated to Salish)
language: Salish (Séliš — Bitterroot Salish dialect); Kootenai (Ksanka — a language isolate with no known relatives)
traditionalTerritories: Flathead Lake basin and the Bitterroot, Mission, Jocko, and Clark Fork river valleys of western Montana. Traditional territories of constituent peoples extend from the Bitterroot Valley north to the Canadian border.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes brings together Bitterroot Salish, Pend d'Oreilles (Qlispé), and Kootenai (Ksanka) peoples. The Kootenai language is a language isolate — completely unrelated to the Salishan languages spoken by the other confederation members. Flathead Lake (Flathead is a misnomer — the people did not practice head flattening) is the largest natural freshwater lake in the western US. The Clark Fork River system connects the Confederated Tribes' territory to the Columbia River salmon system. Traditional Bitterroot Salish territories in the Bitterroot Valley were ceded under the 1855 Hellgate Treaty, a politically fraught history. The confederation has been a model in tribal natural resource management.
heritageSkillRelevance: Flathead Lake and Clark Fork River fishing — bull trout, westslope cutthroat, and Columbia system salmon
Bitterroot (Lewisia rediviva) harvesting and preparation — the Bitterroot Valley is the traditional homeland of this root
Camas harvesting and preparation in Flathead Lake valley meadows
Bitterroot Salish and Kootenai language revitalization programs
Rocky Mountain hunting — bighorn sheep, mountain goat, elk, and bison (historic)
Interior mountain berry harvesting — huckleberry in the Mission Mountains
Tipi construction and Columbia Plateau horse culture — the Salish adopted horses by the early 1700s
culturalSovereigntyNotes: The Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes encompasses three distinct peoples — Bitterroot Salish, Pend d'Oreilles (Qlispé), and Kootenai (Ksanka). The Kootenai language is a complete isolate — not related to Salish. Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or conflate Interior Salish with Coast Salish. Ceremonial protocols for each tradition are Level 3-4.

name: Klamath Tribes
languageFamily: Plateau Penutian (Klamath-Modoc — a Penutian language family of the southern Cascades)
language: Klamath-Modoc (Nqmaqlaq — a Plateau Penutian language distinct from Sahaptin and Chinookan)
traditionalTerritories: Klamath Lake basin, Sprague and Williamson River drainages, and the Upper Klamath River in south-central Oregon and northern California. Upper Klamath Lake is the largest freshwater lake in the Pacific Northwest by surface area.
culturalNotes: The Klamath Tribes brings together Klamath, Modoc, and Yahooskin Band of Snake Paiute peoples in the Klamath Basin. Upper Klamath Lake and the Klamath River system above Klamath Falls were among the most biologically productive wetland environments in the Pacific West — supporting vast populations of waterfowl, suckers (c'waam and koptu fish), and salmon. The Klamath, Modoc, and Yahooskin Paiute were distinct peoples with distinct languages who were consolidated by the 1864 Klamath Treaty. The Klamath Tribes had their federal recognition terminated in 1954 — one of the most significant terminations in US history — and restoration came in 1986. The Klamath River dam removal project (completed 2023-2024) directly affects traditional fishing rights of the Klamath Tribes downstream.
heritageSkillRelevance: Klamath Lake and Williamson/Sprague River sucker fishing — c'waam (shortnose sucker) and koptu (Lost River sucker) are culturally central, currently endangered
Upper Klamath River salmon fishing — chinook historically present in the Klamath system above current dam sites
Waterfowl hunting at Klamath Lake wetlands — one of the premier waterfowl habitats in the Pacific West
Wocas (yellow pond lily) seed harvesting and processing — a distinctive Klamath food technology
Klamath and Modoc language documentation and revitalization efforts
Klamath Basin marsh plant knowledge — tule, cattail, and sedge for mat weaving
High plateau hunting — pronghorn, deer, and elk in the Cascade-Great Basin transition zone
culturalSovereigntyNotes: The Klamath Tribes encompasses three distinct peoples — Klamath, Modoc, and Yahooskin Paiute. Each has distinct language and culture. Klamath-Modoc is a Penutian language — not related to Sahaptin, Chinookan, or Salishan. Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or 'Northwest Coast peoples.' Termination-era historical context is important for teaching this nation's twentieth-century history.

name: Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians
alternateNames: Confederated Tribes of the Siletz Reservation
languageFamily: Multiple — the Siletz confederation includes peoples from numerous language families including Athabascan, Salishan, Penutian, Kalapuyan, and Chinookan
language: Siletz Dee-ni (an Athabascan language); multiple additional languages among constituent peoples
traditionalTerritories: The Siletz Reservation in the Coast Range of Lincoln County, Oregon, represents a consolidation of peoples from many different original territories along the Oregon coast and interior.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Tribes of Siletz Indians is one of the most linguistically and culturally diverse tribal confederations in North America, bringing together peoples from numerous different original territories. In the 1850s-1870s, the US government forcibly relocated peoples from throughout western Oregon to the Coast Reservation (now Siletz). The peoples include Tututni, Coos (Hanis and Miluk), Siuslaw, Umpqua, Kalapuya, Takelma, Shasta, and many others. This forced consolidation means that the Siletz confederation is an administrative political entity preserving multiple distinct cultural traditions. Siletz was another terminated reservation (1954) with recognition restored in 1977.
heritageSkillRelevance: Oregon Coast salmon fishing — multiple traditions for coastal and river fishing across diverse constituent peoples
Tututni (Oregon Athabascan) basket weaving traditions
Oregon Coast marine harvesting — shellfish, sea mammals (historical), and coastal plant knowledge
Siletz River and coastal river salmon and steelhead fishing
Siletz Dee-ni language documentation (Athabascan)
Oregon Coast root and plant food traditions from multiple constituent peoples
Oral history preservation across multiple distinct constituent cultures
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Siletz confederation encompasses dozens of historically distinct peoples — do not treat as culturally homogeneous. When specific cultural attribution is needed, identify the specific constituent people (e.g., Tututni, Coos, Siuslaw) not just 'Siletz.' Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or generic 'Northwest Coast.' Forced relocation history is essential context.

name: Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde
alternateNames: Confederated Tribes of the Grand Ronde Community of Oregon
languageFamily: Multiple — including Kalapuyan, Athabascan, Chinookan, Sahaptian, and Penutian language families among constituent peoples
language: Grand Ronde Chinuk Wawa (a distinct Chinuk Jargon creole revitalized as a first language); multiple ancestral languages among constituent peoples
traditionalTerritories: The Grand Ronde Reservation in the Coast Range of Yamhill County, Oregon consolidates peoples from the Willamette Valley, Coast Range, and adjacent Oregon regions.
culturalNotes: The Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde is another Oregon confederation of forcibly consolidated peoples — including Tualatin, Yamhill, Santiam, and other Kalapuyan peoples, as well as Molala, Athabascan Oregon peoples, Chinookan peoples, and others. Grand Ronde's most remarkable cultural achievement is the revitalization of Chinuk Wawa — a historically pidgin trade language transformed into a vibrant community language with first-language speakers. This language revitalization is internationally recognized as one of the most successful in North America. Willamette Valley camas prairies and the Columbia River salmon system are the ecological anchors of Grand Ronde ancestral territories.
heritageSkillRelevance: Willamette Valley camas harvesting — the Kalapuyan peoples were the primary stewards of the camas prairies
Chinuk Wawa language revitalization — a model for endangered language recovery programs
Willamette River and tributaries salmon and steelhead fishing
Oregon Coast Range plant knowledge from multiple constituent peoples
Kalapuyan basket weaving traditions using Oregon white oak, hazel, and beargrass
Wapato (arrowhead) harvesting in Tualatin Valley wetlands
Fire management for camas prairie maintenance — historical Kalapuyan practice
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Grand Ronde confederation encompasses many historically distinct peoples — do not treat as culturally homogeneous. When cultural attribution is needed, identify specific constituent peoples (Tualatin, Yamhill Kalapuya, Molala, etc.). Chinuk Wawa revitalization is a distinct living language program. Do not aggregate as 'Coast Salish' or 'Northwest Coast.' Forced relocation history is essential teaching context.
